title: Syntax
summary: Learn the small pieces every Java program needs before it can run.
refresher: Java is picky about shape. A basic program lives inside a class, starts running at main, and uses braces to group code. Semicolons end most statements. If one of those pieces is missing, the compiler usually stops before the program ever runs.

blocks.body: Java asks for more structure than Python. Even a tiny program sits inside a class, and the first code that runs goes inside a method named main. At first, that wrapper may feel like extra typing. Think of it as the envelope Java needs before it can read your message.

blocks.caption: The class is the outside box. main is where Java starts. The print line is the one instruction we wrote.

blocks.body: Braces mark where a block starts and ends. The class has its own pair. main has its own pair inside the class. Most beginner syntax errors come from losing one brace, one quote, or one semicolon.

blocks.title: The file name has to match the class name
blocks.body: Python uses indentation to show what belongs together. Java uses braces. Good indentation still matters because it helps your eyes match the braces, but Java follows the braces, not the spaces.

Inside `main` you write statements. A statement is one instruction, and it ends with a semicolon. Java pays no attention to where your lines break. The semicolon is what tells it where one instruction stops and the next one starts, so leaving one off means the compiler cannot find the end of your sentence.
blocks.caption: The broken version forgets the semicolon after println. Java points near the line where it got confused.

With no semicolon, Java keeps waiting for that first instruction to finish. The error it gives you says `';' expected`, and it points at the tail end of the unfinished line, just past the last thing you typed. So when the message mentions a semicolon, read the end of the line the arrow lands on, not the middle of it.
blocks.body: When Java gives a compiler error, read the first one first. Later errors are often just aftershocks from the first missing symbol.

blocks.caption: Curly braces do the other half of the job: they group statements into a block. Count them here. The class opens one, `main` opens another, and the `if` opens a third. The two lines wrapped in the `if` braces run only when the condition holds. The final line is past the closing brace, so the score never affects it.
blocks.output:
Nice work
Keep it up
Done

blocks.title: Braces decide the block, not the spaces
blocks.body: If you are arriving from Python, this is the habit to unlearn first. In Python, indenting a line is what puts it inside the block. In Java, indenting a line does nothing whatsoever. You could squeeze a whole program onto one line and it would still compile. The indentation is there so a human can see the shape of the code, and the braces are what the compiler reads. When the two disagree, the braces win and your eyes lose.

choices: It fails to compile, because the last line is indented wrong
blocks.why: `score` is 50, so `score > 80` is false and "Nice work" is skipped. The last line is indented as though it belonged to the `if`, but the closing brace on the line above already shut that block. The line is part of `main`, not part of the `if`, so it prints for any score at all. If you picked "Nothing at all", you read the indentation the way Python trained you to. Java looks straight past it. Odd indentation is ugly, not illegal, so this compiles fine.

blocks.caption: Two smaller rules. Anything after `//` is a comment, and `/*` opens a comment that runs until `*/`, so both are skipped and one line prints. Java is also case sensitive. Notice the capital S in `System`: type it lowercase and the compiler answers `package system does not exist`, because with no such variable or class in sight it can only read `system` as the name of a package, and there is no package by that name either.

blocks.body: One more difference is worth naming early, because it changes what the word error means. Java is compiled before it runs. The compiler reads your whole file first, and since you announce a type for each variable up front, it can also check that you are using each one sensibly. Handing text to an `int` gets caught right there, at compile time. The program never starts, so nothing above the mistake gets to run either.

blocks.caption: This prints nothing. The compiler sees an `int` being given a string, reports `incompatible types: String cannot be converted to int`, and stops. The `println` on the line above never gets its turn, because there is no run at all, not even a partial one. In Python the same mistake would only surface later, once the bad line was actually reached.

blocks.prompt: This code is saved in a file named `Greeter.java`. Why does it fail to compile?
choices: Two reasons: the `println` statement has no semicolon, and the file name does not match the class name
Just the missing semicolon. The file name is up to you.
Just the file name. A `println` line does not need a semicolon.
blocks.why: Both rules are broken. `System.out.println("Hi")` is a statement, so it needs a semicolon. And a `public class Greeting` has to live in `Greeting.java`, while this file is called `Greeter.java`. Worth knowing: the compiler will not list both at once. It cannot finish reading a file it cannot parse, so it reports the missing semicolon and quits. Only after you add the semicolon and compile again does it get far enough to notice the name and tell you `class Greeting is public, should be declared in a file named Greeting.java`. Two errors, two rounds. That is the normal rhythm of fixing Java: repair the first complaint, compile, see what was hiding behind it.

blocks.title: What to check when Java refuses to compile
items: Class name and file name should match when the class is public.
Most statements end with a semicolon.
Read the first compiler error before changing five things.
